title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
This report analyzes MSBA students’ preferences across the 10 core MSBA courses at UC San Diego Rady School of Management, using data collected via a MaxDiff (Best-Worst Scaling) survey.
What is MaxDiff? MaxDiff is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. Rather than asking respondents to rate items on a numeric scale, MaxDiff presents a small subset of items (here, 4) on each screen and asks respondents to select the most preferred and least preferred item. This repeats over many screens with different item combinations.
Why MaxDiff instead of a rating scale? Humans are good at trade-off judgments — picking the best and worst from a small set — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales consistently. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and eliminates scale-use bias.
Goals: We estimate students’ relative preference for each of the 10 labeled MSBA classes using three methods:

A simple counts analysis — % times picked best minus % times picked worst
The MNL model fit by maximum likelihood (MLE) — a probabilistic model that uses all the data more efficiently
The same MNL model fit by Bayesian MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings) — which adds principled uncertainty quantification

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
The Multinomial Logit (MNL) model assigns each class \(j\) a latent utility \(\beta_j\). On a task showing items \(\{a,b,c,d\}\), the probability item \(b\) is picked best is:
\[P(\text{best}=b) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j)}\]
After the best is removed, the probability item \(c\) is picked worst (negated utilities):
\[P(\text{worst}=c \mid \text{best}=b) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{b\}} \exp(-\beta_j)}\]
Each task yields two MNL observations. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
def build_tasks(df):
    tasks = []
    for (rid, t), g in df.groupby(["record_id", "task"]):
        b = g[g["response"] ==  1]["item"].values
        w = g[g["response"] == -1]["item"].values
        if len(b) == 1 and len(w) == 1:
            tasks.append({"items": g["item"].tolist(),
                          "best":  int(b[0]),
                          "worst": int(w[0])})
    return tasks

tasks = build_tasks(df)
print(f"Tasks: {len(tasks)}  |  MNL observations: {len(tasks)*2}")


Tasks: 680  |  MNL observations: 1360

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
\[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \left[ \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}} \exp(-\beta_j) \right]\]


Code
from scipy.optimize import minimize

def neg_ll(beta_free, tasks):
    beta  = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free])
    total = 0.0
    for t in tasks:
        items = np.array(t["items"]) - 1
        best  = t["best"]  - 1
        worst = t["worst"] - 1
        b     = beta[items]
        # numerically stable log-sum-exp
        total += beta[best]  - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b - b.max()))) - b.max()
        rem    = items[items != best]
        b_rem  = -beta[rem]
        total += -beta[worst] - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b_rem - b_rem.max()))) - b_rem.max()
    return -total

# Two-stage: Nelder-Mead warm start → BFGS
res0 = minimize(neg_ll, np.zeros(9), args=(tasks,), method="Nelder-Mead",
                options={"maxiter": 20000, "xatol": 1e-9, "fatol": 1e-9})
res  = minimize(neg_ll, res0.x, args=(tasks,), method="BFGS",
                options={"maxiter": 5000, "gtol": 1e-7})

beta_hat   = np.concatenate([[0.0], res.x])
se_hat     = np.concatenate([[np.nan], np.sqrt(np.diag(res.hess_inv))])
shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum()

print(f"Log-likelihood at optimum: {-res.fun:.2f}")

mle_df = labels_raw.copy()
mle_df["beta"]  = beta_hat
mle_df["se"]    = se_hat
mle_df["share"] = shares_mle
mle_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False)[["item","short","beta","se","share"]].round(4)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
Prior: \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\). Posterior: \(\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \propto L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})\)
We use the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm to draw from the posterior.


Code
def log_post(beta_free, tasks):
    return -neg_ll(beta_free, tasks) + np.sum(-0.5 * beta_free**2 / 10.0)

def mh_sampler(beta0, n_iter, step, tasks, seed=42):
    np.random.seed(seed)
    K      = len(beta0)
    draws  = np.zeros((n_iter, K))
    beta   = beta0.copy()
    lp     = log_post(beta, tasks)
    accept = 0
    for t in range(n_iter):
        beta_star = beta + np.random.randn(K) * step
        lp_star   = log_post(beta_star, tasks)
        if np.log(np.random.rand()) < lp_star - lp:
            beta   = beta_star
            lp     = lp_star
            accept += 1
        draws[t] = beta
    return draws, accept / n_iter

draws, accept_rate = mh_sampler(res.x.copy(), n_iter=20000, step=0.08, tasks=tasks)

burn_in    = 5000
post_draws = draws[burn_in:]          # 15,000 post-burn-in draws
print(f"Acceptance rate: {accept_rate:.1%}  (target: 25–40%)")


Acceptance rate: 29.1%  (target: 25–40%)

for ax, idx, name in zip(axes, param_idx, param_names):
    ax.plot(post_draws[:, idx], linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.8, color="#2166ac")
    ax.axhline(post_draws[:, idx].mean(), color="red", linewidth=1,
               linestyle="--", alpha=0.7, label="Posterior mean")

Minor disagreements in the middle reflect the fact that when utilities are similar, small statistical noise can flip adjacent positions.
The MNL adds over counts by treating every unselected item as informative. Bayes adds over MLE by providing uncertainty estimates on derived quantities (shares) without the delta method, and it can be extended to hierarchical models for individual-level preferences.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion
Substantive findings: MSBA students strongly prefer applied, analytically creative courses. Customer Analytics and Marketing Analytics top all rankings, reflecting demand for immediately marketable skills. AI Math & Programming ranks last — perhaps perceived as the most demanding without offering the same creative payoff. The summer SQL course is notably an exception: students appreciate it despite the compressed timeline.
Methodological takeaways:

Counts: Fast and intuitive, but ignores choice-set structure.
MLE MNL: Rigorous point estimates; every unselected item contributes information.
Bayesian MNL: Full posterior uncertainty on derived quantities; extends naturally to hierarchical models.

Caveats: Self-selected sample at one institution; results may not generalize. Preferences likely vary with prior experience and program stage.
Next steps: A hierarchical Bayesian MNL (mixed logit) would reveal individual-level heterogeneity — for example, whether students with quantitative backgrounds have systematically different preferences.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
This report analyzes MSBA students’ preferences across the 10 core MSBA courses at UC San Diego Rady School of Management, using data collected via a MaxDiff (Best-Worst Scaling) survey.
What is MaxDiff? MaxDiff is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. Rather than asking respondents to rate items on a numeric scale, MaxDiff presents a small subset of items (here, 4) on each screen and asks respondents to select the most preferred and least preferred item. This repeats over many screens with different item combinations.
Why MaxDiff instead of a rating scale? Humans are good at trade-off judgments — picking the best and worst from a small set — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales consistently. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and eliminates scale-use bias.
Goals: We estimate students’ relative preference for each of the 10 labeled MSBA classes using three methods of increasing sophistication:

A simple counts analysis — % times picked best minus % times picked worst
The MNL model fit by maximum likelihood (MLE) — a probabilistic model that uses all the data more efficiently
The same MNL model fit by Bayesian MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings) — which adds principled uncertainty quantification

All three methods should broadly agree on the ranking; they differ in statistical rigor and how they communicate uncertainty.

Counts Analysis
The simplest summary of MaxDiff data is a counts analysis. For each class \(j\), we compute the percentage of times it was picked as best, the percentage picked as worst, and take the difference:
\[\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j\]
A score near +1 means the class is almost always the most preferred when shown; near −1 means almost always least preferred; near 0 means middle-of-the-pack.

)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting a model, we need to understand how MaxDiff responses map to MNL choice observations.
The Multinomial Logit (MNL) model assigns each class \(j\) a latent utility coefficient \(\beta_j\). On a task showing items \(\{a, b, c, d\}\), the probability item \(b\) is picked best is the softmax:
\[P(\text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\exp(\beta_a) + \exp(\beta_b) + \exp(\beta_c) + \exp(\beta_d)}\]
After the best item is removed, the probability item \(c\) is picked worst — using negated utilities since we are picking the least preferred — is:
\[P(\text{worst} = c \mid \text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\exp(-\beta_a) + \exp(-\beta_c) + \exp(-\beta_d)}\]
Each task therefore yields two MNL observations: one for the best-pick from all 4 items, and one for the worst-pick from the remaining 3.
Identifiability: The softmax only depends on differences between utilities — adding the same constant to every \(\beta\) leaves all probabilities unchanged. We can only identify \(J - 1 = 9\) coefficients. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (AI Math & Programming as reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
def build_tasks(df):
    tasks = []
    for (_, t), g in df.groupby(["record_id", "task"]):
        b = g[g["response"] ==  1]["item"].values
        w = g[g["response"] == -1]["item"].values
        if len(b) == 1 and len(w) == 1:
            tasks.append({"items": g["item"].tolist(),
                          "best":  int(b[0]),
                          "worst": int(w[0])})
    return tasks

tasks = build_tasks(df)
print(f"Tasks: {len(tasks)}  |  MNL observations: {len(tasks) * 2}")


Tasks: 680  |  MNL observations: 1360

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The log-likelihood summed over all tasks is:
\[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \left[ \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}} \exp(-\beta_j) \right]\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in each task.


Code
from scipy.optimize import minimize

def neg_ll(beta_free, tasks):
    beta  = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free])
    total = 0.0
    for t in tasks:
        items = np.array(t["items"]) - 1
        best  = t["best"]  - 1
        worst = t["worst"] - 1
        b     = beta[items]
        # numerically stable log-sum-exp
        total += beta[best]  - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b - b.max()))) - b.max()
        rem    = items[items != best]
        b_rem  = -beta[rem]
        total += -beta[worst] - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b_rem - b_rem.max()))) - b_rem.max()
    return -total

# Two-stage optimization: Nelder-Mead warm start → BFGS
res0 = minimize(neg_ll, np.zeros(9), args=(tasks,), method="Nelder-Mead",
                options={"maxiter": 20000, "xatol": 1e-9, "fatol": 1e-9})
res  = minimize(neg_ll, res0.x, args=(tasks,), method="BFGS",
                options={"maxiter": 5000, "gtol": 1e-7})

beta_hat   = np.concatenate([[0.0], res.x])
se_hat     = np.concatenate([[np.nan], np.sqrt(np.diag(res.hess_inv))])
shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum()

print(f"Log-likelihood at optimum: {-res.fun:.2f}")

mle_df = labels_raw.copy()
mle_df["beta"]  = beta_hat
mle_df["se"]    = se_hat
mle_df["share"] = shares_mle
mle_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False)[["item", "short", "beta", "se", "share"]].round(4)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
We now fit the same MNL model using Bayesian methods. The posterior is:
\[\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \propto L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})\]
We use a weakly informative prior \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\), which places a standard deviation of \(\approx 3.2\) on each coefficient — diffuse enough not to distort the likelihood but informative enough to regularize extreme estimates.


Code
def log_post(beta_free, tasks):
    return -neg_ll(beta_free, tasks) + np.sum(-0.5 * beta_free**2 / 10.0)

def mh_sampler(beta0, n_iter, step, tasks, seed=42):
    np.random.seed(seed)
    K      = len(beta0)
    draws  = np.zeros((n_iter, K))
    beta   = beta0.copy()
    lp     = log_post(beta, tasks)
    accept = 0
    for t in range(n_iter):
        beta_star = beta + np.random.randn(K) * step
        lp_star   = log_post(beta_star, tasks)
        if np.log(np.random.rand()) < lp_star - lp:
            beta   = beta_star
            lp     = lp_star
            accept += 1
        draws[t] = beta
    return draws, accept / n_iter




Code
# Start from MLE estimates for efficiency
# Step size tuned to achieve ~25-40% acceptance rate:
# step=0.04 → ~55% (too high), step=0.15 → ~12% (too low), step=0.08 → ~28% (good)
draws, accept_rate = mh_sampler(res.x.copy(), n_iter=20000, step=0.08, tasks=tasks)

burn_in    = 5000  # discard first 25%
post_draws = draws[burn_in:]
print(f"Acceptance rate: {accept_rate:.1%}  (target: 25–40%)")
print(f"Post-burn-in draws: {len(post_draws):,}")


Acceptance rate: 29.1%  (target: 25–40%)
Post-burn-in draws: 15,000


The step size of 0.08 was tuned to yield an acceptance rate of approximately 28%, within the recommended 25–40% range. A step that is too large leads to frequent rejections and poor exploration; too small leads to slow mixing despite high acceptance.


Code

param_names = ["β₂ (SQL)", "β₅ (Biz Analytics)", "β₉ (Mktg Analytics)"]

for ax, idx, name in zip(axes, param_idx, param_names):
    ax.plot(post_draws[:, idx], linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.8, color="#2166ac")
    ax.axhline(post_draws[:, idx].mean(), color="red", linewidth=1,
               linestyle="--", alpha=0.7)
    ax.set_ylabel(name, fontsize=10)

axes[-1].set_xlabel("Post-Burn-In Iteration")

                  fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")

post_beta_full = np.hstack([np.zeros((len(post_draws), 1)), post_draws])

post_mean = post_beta_full.mean(axis=0)
post_lo   = np.percentile(post_beta_full, 2.5,  axis=0)

def softmax(x):
    e = np.exp(x - x.max())
    return e / e.sum()

shares_draws = np.apply_along_axis(softmax, 1, post_beta_full)

Bayesian posterior mean shares of preference with 95% credible intervals.




Bayes vs. MLE comparison: The posterior means are very close to the MLE point estimates — typically within 0.05 of each other — and the 95% credible intervals are nearly identical in width to ±1.96 × SE from MLE. The small differences are due to the prior gently pulling estimates toward zero (regularization), which matters most for items at the extremes or with fewer appearances. The main advantage of the Bayesian approach is not a different central estimate, but rather automatic uncertainty quantification on derived quantities: to get credible intervals on shares of preference, we simply push each MCMC draw through the softmax and take quantiles — no delta method required.

Minor disagreements appear in the middle, where items have similar utilities and small statistical noise can flip adjacent positions.
The MNL adds over counts by modeling the full choice process: every unselected item contributes information about not being picked, not just the items that were chosen. This makes the estimates more efficient.
The Bayesian approach adds over MLE by providing credible intervals on derived quantities like shares of preference — we simply push each MCMC draw through the softmax and take quantiles, with no delta method required. It also opens the door to hierarchical extensions that estimate individual-level preferences.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion
Substantive findings: MSBA students strongly prefer applied, analytically creative courses. Marketing Analytics (495) and Customer Analytics (455) top all three rankings, reflecting demand for skills directly tied to business decisions. AI Math & Programming (403) consistently ranks last — perhaps perceived as the most mathematically demanding without offering the same immediate creative payoff. SQL (464) is a notable exception among the summer boot camp courses: students appreciate its practical utility despite the compressed timeline.
Methodological takeaways:

Counts: Fast and intuitive, ideal for a quick executive dashboard. Limitation: ignores the structure of choice sets.
MLE MNL: Rigorous point estimates with standard errors. Every unselected item contributes information. Good when a single defensible number is needed.
Bayesian MNL: Full posterior uncertainty on derived quantities; extends naturally to hierarchical models for individual-level preference heterogeneity.

Caveats: This is a self-selected sample of MSBA students at one institution. Preferences likely depend on where students are in the program, prior work experience, and current course load. Results may not generalize beyond this group.
Next steps: A hierarchical Bayesian MNL (mixed logit) would reveal individual-level heterogeneity — for example, whether students with quantitative backgrounds have systematically different preferences from those with business or social science backgrounds.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
This report analyzes MSBA students’ preferences across the 10 core MSBA courses at UC San Diego Rady School of Management, using data collected via a MaxDiff (Best-Worst Scaling) survey.
What is MaxDiff? MaxDiff is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. Rather than asking respondents to rate items on a numeric scale, MaxDiff presents a small subset of items (here, 4) on each screen and asks respondents to select the most preferred and least preferred item. This repeats over many screens with different item combinations.
Why MaxDiff instead of a rating scale? Humans are good at trade-off judgments — picking the best and worst from a small set — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales consistently. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and eliminates scale-use bias.
Goals: We estimate students’ relative preference for each of the 10 labeled MSBA classes using three methods of increasing sophistication:

A simple counts analysis — % times picked best minus % times picked worst
The MNL model fit by maximum likelihood (MLE) — a probabilistic model that uses all the data more efficiently
The same MNL model fit by Bayesian MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings) — which adds principled uncertainty quantification

All three methods should broadly agree on the ranking; they differ in statistical rigor and how they communicate uncertainty.

text:
Counts Analysis
The simplest summary of MaxDiff data is a counts analysis. For each class \(j\), we compute the percentage of times it was picked as best, the percentage picked as worst, and take the difference:
\[\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j\]
A score near +1 means the class is almost always the most preferred when shown; near −1 means almost always least preferred; near 0 means middle-of-the-pack.

    .reset_index()
)
counts["pct_best"]  = counts["times_best"]  / counts["times_shown"]
counts["pct_worst"] = counts["times_worst"] / counts["times_shown"]

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting a model, we need to understand how MaxDiff responses map to MNL choice observations.
The Multinomial Logit (MNL) model assigns each class \(j\) a latent utility coefficient \(\beta_j\). On a task showing items \(\{a, b, c, d\}\), the probability item \(b\) is picked best is the softmax:
\[P(\text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\exp(\beta_a) + \exp(\beta_b) + \exp(\beta_c) + \exp(\beta_d)}\]
After the best item is removed, the probability item \(c\) is picked worst — using negated utilities since we are picking the least preferred — is:
\[P(\text{worst} = c \mid \text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\exp(-\beta_a) + \exp(-\beta_c) + \exp(-\beta_d)}\]
Each task therefore yields two MNL observations: one for the best-pick from all 4 items, and one for the worst-pick from the remaining 3.
Identifiability: The softmax only depends on differences between utilities — adding the same constant to every \(\beta\) leaves all probabilities unchanged. We can only identify \(J - 1 = 9\) coefficients. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (AI Math & Programming as reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
def build_tasks(df):
    tasks = []
    for (_, t), g in df.groupby(["record_id", "task"]):
        b = g[g["response"] ==  1]["item"].values
        w = g[g["response"] == -1]["item"].values
        if len(b) == 1 and len(w) == 1:
            tasks.append({"items": g["item"].tolist(),
                          "best":  int(b[0]),
                          "worst": int(w[0])})
    return tasks

tasks = build_tasks(df)
print(f"Tasks: {len(tasks)}  |  MNL observations: {len(tasks) * 2}")


Tasks: 680  |  MNL observations: 1360

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The log-likelihood summed over all tasks is:
\[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \left[ \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown}} \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}} \exp(-\beta_j) \right]\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in each task.


Code
from scipy.optimize import minimize

def neg_ll(beta_free, tasks):
    beta  = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free])
    total = 0.0
    for t in tasks:
        items = np.array(t["items"]) - 1
        best  = t["best"]  - 1
        worst = t["worst"] - 1
        b     = beta[items]
        # numerically stable log-sum-exp
        total += beta[best]  - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b - b.max()))) - b.max()
        rem    = items[items != best]
        b_rem  = -beta[rem]
        total += -beta[worst] - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(b_rem - b_rem.max()))) - b_rem.max()
    return -total

# Two-stage optimization: Nelder-Mead warm start → BFGS
res0 = minimize(neg_ll, np.zeros(9), args=(tasks,), method="Nelder-Mead",
                options={"maxiter": 20000, "xatol": 1e-9, "fatol": 1e-9})
res  = minimize(neg_ll, res0.x, args=(tasks,), method="BFGS",
                options={"maxiter": 5000, "gtol": 1e-7})

beta_hat   = np.concatenate([[0.0], res.x])
se_hat     = np.concatenate([[np.nan], np.sqrt(np.diag(res.hess_inv))])
shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum()

print(f"Log-likelihood at optimum: {-res.fun:.2f}")

mle_df = labels_raw.copy()
mle_df["beta"]  = beta_hat
mle_df["se"]    = se_hat
mle_df["share"] = shares_mle
mle_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False)[["item", "short", "beta", "se", "share"]].round(4)

The shares of preference \(\hat{s}_j = \exp(\hat{\beta}_j) / \sum_k \exp(\hat{\beta}_k)\) convert raw \(\beta\)’s into an interpretable scale: if all 10 classes were shown on a single screen, what fraction of choices would each receive?

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
We now fit the same MNL model using Bayesian methods. The posterior is:
\[\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \propto L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})\]
We use a weakly informative prior \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\), which places a standard deviation of \(\approx 3.2\) on each coefficient — diffuse enough not to distort the likelihood but informative enough to regularize extreme estimates.


Code
def log_post(beta_free, tasks):
    return -neg_ll(beta_free, tasks) + np.sum(-0.5 * beta_free**2 / 10.0)

def mh_sampler(beta0, n_iter, step, tasks, seed=42):
    np.random.seed(seed)
    K      = len(beta0)
    draws  = np.zeros((n_iter, K))
    beta   = beta0.copy()
    lp     = log_post(beta, tasks)
    accept = 0
    for t in range(n_iter):
        beta_star = beta + np.random.randn(K) * step
        lp_star   = log_post(beta_star, tasks)
        if np.log(np.random.rand()) < lp_star - lp:
            beta   = beta_star
            lp     = lp_star
            accept += 1
        draws[t] = beta
    return draws, accept / n_iter




Code
# Start from MLE estimates for efficiency
# Step size tuned to achieve ~25-40% acceptance rate:
# step=0.04 → ~55% (too high), step=0.15 → ~12% (too low), step=0.08 → ~28% (good)
draws, accept_rate = mh_sampler(res.x.copy(), n_iter=20000, step=0.08, tasks=tasks)

burn_in    = 5000  # discard first 25%
post_draws = draws[burn_in:]
print(f"Acceptance rate: {accept_rate:.1%}  (target: 25–40%)")
print(f"Post-burn-in draws: {len(post_draws):,}")


Acceptance rate: 29.1%  (target: 25–40%)
Post-burn-in draws: 15,000


The step size of 0.08 was tuned to yield an acceptance rate of approximately 28%, within the recommended 25–40% range. A step that is too large leads to frequent rejections and poor exploration; too small leads to slow mixing despite high acceptance.

Bayesian posterior mean shares of preference with 95% credible intervals.




Bayes vs. MLE comparison: The posterior means are very close to the MLE point estimates — typically within 0.05 of each other — and the 95% credible intervals are nearly identical in width to ±1.96 × SE from MLE. The small differences are due to the prior gently pulling estimates toward zero (regularization), which matters most for items at the extremes or with fewer appearances. The main advantage of the Bayesian approach is not a different central estimate, but rather automatic uncertainty quantification on derived quantities: to get credible intervals on shares of preference, we simply push each MCMC draw through the softmax and take quantiles — no delta method required.

Discussion: All three methods agree strongly at the extremes — Marketing Analytics (495) and Customer Analytics (455) are consistently top-ranked; AI Math & Programming (403) and Analytics Consulting (444) consistently bottom-ranked. Minor disagreements appear in the middle, where items have similar utilities and small statistical noise can flip adjacent positions.
The MNL adds over counts by modeling the full choice process: every unselected item contributes information about not being picked, not just the items that were chosen. This makes the estimates more efficient.
The Bayesian approach adds over MLE by providing credible intervals on derived quantities like shares of preference — we simply push each MCMC draw through the softmax and take quantiles, with no delta method required. It also opens the door to hierarchical extensions that estimate individual-level preferences.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion
Substantive findings: MSBA students strongly prefer applied, analytically creative courses. Marketing Analytics (495) and Customer Analytics (455) top all three rankings, reflecting demand for skills directly tied to business decisions. AI Math & Programming (403) consistently ranks last — perhaps perceived as the most mathematically demanding without offering the same immediate creative payoff. SQL (464) is a notable exception among the summer boot camp courses: students appreciate its practical utility despite the compressed timeline.
Methodological takeaways:

Counts: Fast and intuitive, ideal for a quick executive dashboard. Limitation: ignores the structure of choice sets.
MLE MNL: Rigorous point estimates with standard errors. Every unselected item contributes information. Good when a single defensible number is needed.
Bayesian MNL: Full posterior uncertainty on derived quantities; extends naturally to hierarchical models for individual-level preference heterogeneity.

Caveats: This is a self-selected sample of MSBA students at one institution. Preferences likely depend on where students are in the program, prior work experience, and current course load. Results may not generalize beyond this group.
Next steps: A hierarchical Bayesian MNL (mixed logit) would reveal individual-level heterogeneity — for example, whether students with quantitative backgrounds have systematically different preferences from those with business or social science backgrounds.
